one_line_takeaway: CAC is a transfer-based black-box adversarial attack that alternates surrogate distillation with white-box attacks on a contracting search space, and is the first such method to come with a proven bound on the number of iterations to find a transferable adversarial example, while also empirically producing smaller-norm perturbations than HopSkipJump, SignOPT, GeoDA, SquareAttack, SparseRS, PAR, and AdvViT on ImageNet/CIFAR-10 against ResNet-50 and ViT-B.
key_finding: Across all 4 dataset/model combinations x 2 label-access settings (8 tables total), CAC achieves ASR=1.00 in every hard-label setting and every soft-label setting except CIFAR-10 ResNet-50 hard-label baselines partially lower (e.g. SignOPT 0.92, GeoDA 0.96) — CAC itself is 1.00 there too. Concretely: ResNet-50/ImageNet hard-label, CAC gets ASR=1.00, AQN=487.95, avg l2=35.074, avg l_inf=0.153, beating HopSkipJump-l2 (l2=48.838, l_inf=0.539), HopSkipJump-l_inf (l2=73.255, l_inf=0.361), SignOPT (l2=48.047, l_inf=0.551), GeoDA (l2=49.658, l_inf=0.180). On ViT-B/ImageNet hard-label, CAC (l_inf=0.165) beats all compared methods including PAR (l_inf=0.889) and AdvViT, the latter of which only reaches ASR=0.75 vs CAC's 1.00. In soft-label settings the gap is largest on ViT-B/ImageNet: CAC ASR=1.00 vs SquareAttack-l_inf ASR=0.26 and SparseRS ASR=0.79. The paper's own summary claim (Section 4.2) is that CAC yields adversarial examples closer to target points than baselines in nearly all l_inf comparisons and most l2 comparisons, while being the only method with a formal convergence guarantee (baselines show comparably high ASR empirically but no such guarantee).
core_contribution: The paper proposes Contract And Conquer (CAC), an iterative transfer-based black-box adversarial attack that alternates (1) knowledge distillation of the black-box target model T into a small white-box surrogate model S trained on an expanding distillation dataset D(S), and (2) a white-box MI-FGSM attack on S within a search space Uδ(x) that is contracted after each failed transfer attempt (radius ρj = t·||zj − zj-1||∞ around the latest candidate). If a computed adversarial example zj transfers from S to T (verified by one query to T), the algorithm returns it; otherwise (zj, T(zj)) is added to D(S), the search space shrinks, and the process repeats. The paper proves (Lemma 3.4, proof in Appendix A) that under stated assumptions on the surrogate model's fidelity/confidence (Eq. 4) and bounded-gradient smoothness (Eq. 8), CAC is guaranteed to produce a transferable adversarial example within a bounded number of alternation iterations n, where (n-1) ln t <= ln ε - ln δ - ln γ. This convergence guarantee, not just an empirical attack, is presented as the paper's central novelty over existing black-box attacks.
threat_model: Black-box evasion attack on image classifiers. The adversary has only query access to a target model T (hard-label or soft-label output) and no access to weights, gradients, or architecture. The attacker seeks an l_infinity-bounded (||x - x'||_inf <= δ) adversarial perturbation (untargeted, per Definition 3.1) at a correctly-classified input x, using a bounded query budget (500 queries per target point in the main experiments, 300 for ResNet-50/CIFAR-10). The attack is transfer-based: it builds and repeatedly refines a surrogate model S in a white-box setting, querying T only to (a) label points added to the distillation set and (b) test transferability of each candidate adversarial example.
limitations: The convergence guarantee (Lemma 3.4) rests on assumptions the authors themselves flag as only 'practically verifiable,' not provably satisfied in general: (1) the surrogate model must have bounded gradients everywhere in Uδ(x) at every iteration (Eq. 8), achieved only by construction using layers/activations with bounded gradients; (2) the surrogate must match the target's predictions with a confidence margin > ε on the distillation set at every iteration (Eq. 4), which is an empirical training outcome, not something guaranteed by the distillation procedure itself; (3) the lemma additionally assumes the white-box attack always succeeds in producing an adversarial example for S at every iteration, which MI-FGSM is not guaranteed to do. The paper's own Future Work section acknowledges the goal of 'reduction of the influence of practical assumptions, specifically, the possibility to compute an adversarial example for the surrogate model on each algorithm iteration.' Each alternation iteration requires retraining the ResNet-18 surrogate for 100 epochs, which is a real per-query computational cost not reported in wall-clock terms. Evaluation is limited to image classification (CIFAR-10, ImageNet) with only two target architectures (ResNet-50, ViT-B) and 100 target points per setting; no results on other modalities (text, audio) or larger-scale/production black-box APIs are given despite such settings being mentioned in the introduction as motivation.

quantitative_results: ResNet-50/ImageNet hard-label: CAC ASR=1.00, AQN=487.95, avg l2=35.074, avg l_inf=0.153
ViT-B/ImageNet hard-label: CAC ASR=1.00, AQN=488.91, avg l2=49.282, avg l_inf=0.165; AdvViT ASR=0.75 (only baseline below 1.00 ASR)
ResNet-50/ImageNet soft-label: CAC ASR=1.00, avg l_inf=0.122; SquareAttack-l_inf ASR=0.98, avg l_inf=0.250; SparseRS ASR=0.94, avg l_inf=0.994
ViT-B/ImageNet soft-label: CAC ASR=1.00, avg l_inf=0.144; SquareAttack-l_inf ASR=0.26, avg l_inf=0.250; SparseRS ASR=0.79, avg l_inf=0.993
ResNet-50/CIFAR-10 hard-label: CAC ASR=1.00, AQN=291.0, avg l2=2.675, avg l_inf=0.061; SignOPT ASR=0.92; GeoDA ASR=0.96
ViT-B/CIFAR-10 hard-label: CAC ASR=1.00, avg l_inf=0.070; HopSkipJump-l2 ASR=0.99; SignOPT ASR=0.96; GeoDA ASR=0.94
Target model clean accuracies: ResNet-50 ImageNet 80.13%, ResNet-50 CIFAR-10 94.65%, ViT-B ImageNet 85.21%, ViT-B CIFAR-10 96.89%
Convergence bound: (n-1) ln t <= ln ε - ln δ - ln γ, with experimental hyperparameters δ=0.125, t=0.99, surrogate=ResNet-18, distillation set size m=300, MI-FGSM iterations M=3
